source.title: Over the river, through the woods: cognitive maps in the hippocampus and orbitofrontal cortex

confidence_rationale: Wikenheiser and Schoenbaum argue that the hippocampus maintains not just spatial maps but relational cognitive maps that encode associations between cues, actions, outcomes, and their values. They review evidence that hippocampal representations are updated when reward structure changes, and that HPC-OFC interaction allows value information to be integrated into the spatial/relational map. This is directly relevant to MECH-099's claim that the residue field encodes accumulated harm/benefit history -- the Wikenheiser-Schoenbaum framing provides the theoretical justification that hippocampal maps are value-annotated, not purely geometric. The review is from Nature Reviews Neuroscience (high quality) and is widely influential. Confidence docked slightly because this is a review/theory piece, not a direct empirical demonstration of persistent harm accumulation.
failure_signatures: The review addresses value in the context of reward and goal outcomes, not harm accumulation specifically -- the harm/aversive dimension is underrepresented relative to reward.
As a review paper, it does not provide direct experimental data on how the spatial map encodes harm history.
The OFC involvement they argue for is not modelled explicitly in REE's V3 architecture -- the hippocampus-OFC loop maps only partially onto REE's E3/hippocampal system.

mapping.source_claim_statement: The hippocampus and OFC both maintain cognitive maps that encode complex relationships between cues, actions, outcomes, and values. The hippocampal map is updated flexibly when the reward structure changes, allowing organisms to navigate toward value without complete re-learning. HPC and OFC contribute complementary map content: HPC provides the structural/spatial scaffold; OFC provides value annotations.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-099 posits that the E3 hippocampal system maintains a residue field that is a persistent spatial map of harm and benefit accumulation -- not just current signals but history. The Wikenheiser-Schoenbaum review provides the theoretical grounding for this: if hippocampal maps encode value relationships flexibly (not just geometry), then the accumulation of harm/benefit history over time is a natural extension of the same map-updating mechanism they document. The parallel with OFC as value annotator maps onto the E3 harm/goal error signal that writes to the residue field -- the hippocampal spatial scaffold is the field geometry; the harm/benefit signal from E3's evaluation is the OFC analog that annotates it.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The value integration in Wikenheiser-Schoenbaum is primarily about reward, not harm. REE's residue field is primarily a harm-tracking structure. The asymmetry between harm and reward processing (documented in the amygdala literature) means the hippocampus may treat negative-valence value differently than the review implies. The OFC analog mapping is architectural speculation at this stage.
mapping.source_context: Review of hippocampal and OFC cognitive map literature; integrates place cell, theta sequence, and reward learning paradigms; proposes parallel HPC-OFC map interactions.

confidence_components.notes: High source quality (Nature Reviews Neuroscience, senior authors). Mapping fidelity moderate: the value-in-maps concept directly supports MECH-099, but the harm/accumulation specifics are not addressed. Transfer risk moderate: the review is rodent-centric and reward-focused.
